text:
This monograph offers a comprehensive revaluation of Polish-Moldavian relations during the pivotal transition from the Middle Ages to the early modern period (1492–1538). While previous historiography has largely focused on isolated military engagements, this study situates diplomacy within a rigorous geopolitical framework, examining the intersection of interests between the Kingdom of Poland, the Principality of Moldavia, the Kingdom of Hungary, and the Ottoman Empire.

The narrative spans the reigns of Jan Olbracht, Alexander Jagiellon, and Sigismund I the Old, tracing the evolution of Polish policy toward the Moldavian state. Key focus areas include the political-legal status of Moldavia under Stephen the Great, the ideological influence of humanism and the "Antemurale Christianitatis" (Bulwark of Christendom) concept on Jagiellonian strategy, and the significant role of powerful noble families, such as the Chodeckis, in shaping frontier conflicts. Central to the work is an analysis of the persistent dispute over the Pokuttya region and the shifting alliances prompted by the Hungarian defeat at Mohács and the subsequent rise of Petru Rareș.

Grounded in extensive archival research across Poland, Romania, Austria, Hungary, and Germany, the book utilizes previously untapped diplomatic correspondence and treaty documents to provide a multi-perspective view of regional power dynamics. By moving beyond a purely Polonocentric narrative, the author demonstrates how Moldavia served as a crucial diplomatic theater where the ambitions of the Jagiellonians, the Habsburgs, and the Sublime Porte collided. The study concludes in 1538, a year marked by the consolidation of Ottoman suzerainty over Moldavia and the definitive transformation of the East-Central European political landscape.
